meta.place: Malthouse Press, Nigeria, African Books Collective

text.p: Battles are generally won with morale. How morale is got varies. To most of the civil populace, the justification of going to war is morale-boosting. Everyday, the government used every means to propagate lucid pictures depicting the opponent as a vampire, devil, vandal, cannibal and what-have-you. To the fighting men in the fronts, propaganda was useless as a morale-boosting factor. It was not the justness of the national cause that spurred the fighting men to gallant deeds. Though the Federal cause was succinctly put during the civil war, almost every young man that enlisted at that period had his personal reasons. The national cause had little to do as far as most of us were concerned.

Many young men, for instance, enlisted so as to avenge humiliations meted out on them or members of their families by soldiers. In most cases, the avengers knew that they were not going to redeem the humiliations on the soldier that had caused it, but on other innocent civilians. It was a vicious cycle in which the stay-at-homes were always at the receiving end. Sometimes, it was not always for vendetta, but for self-assertion. Most young men felt that they would become supermen once they enlisted in the army. They believed that once a soldier, nobody could dictate to them again. How true this belief is, is a matter of individual experience. For, the humiliating acts passing from one soldier to another is, in most cases, much deeper than from soldier to civilians.

To many other young men, the glamour of the soldiers' uniform was an irresistible factor that lured many lads into the army. The privileges, real and imagined, and the respect or awe associated with the soldier were some of the other factors that lured many lads into the army. Finally, there were those who enlisted as a result of unemployment or dissatisfaction with jobs held. For instance, those of us who enlisted from the Mid-west after the Biafran invasions were not necessarily the most outraged. Our signing up was no doubt fuelled by some of the aforementioned factors.

After enlistment, the pride, to live up to personal or external expectations of the profession was usually a driving force behind many heroic exploits. The pride of where one was trained and the unit one belonged to was usually encouraged by military traditions. So as not to let down their alma mater or the renowned unit to which they belonged, many soldiers performed the near impossible. If battles were to be football matches and the spectators civilians, every soldier will probably end up a martyr. To have run battles watched by the sub-humans known as civilians would have been unthinkable. All battles would have been fought to the last man. Many Senior Officers parading about with their exploits as commanders during the war will surely commit suicide if a video recording of their roles in battles were made available.

Since there are no civilian spectators during battles, when professional pride is confronted by the stark realities of wars, it is only morale that can propel the fighting men to greater sacrifices. And nothing gives morale to the men in battle more than previous victories. Until they have been used in scoring victories, superior training, numbers and equipment are nothing. A bow and arrow carrying unit that was able to defeat a conventionally well-equipped modern opponent is bound to have high morale. With that initial victory-induced high morale, the bow and arrow unit is likely to continue defeating its rifle-carrying opponent. Greater number of men, better weapons and first class training cannot win any battle if the morale is zero.

By the time some of us were drafted back to retake our lost rear, the series of defeats suffered by our men at Umuagbai had reduced our pride and morale. This was aggravated by the absence of Lt. Akumoko who did not accompany us. With such a low morale, and in the absence of our trusted Officer, things seemed bound to go haywire. The sophisticated weapons which we had not seen before, but which were thrown in to assist us to retake Umuagbai were of little help. The only thing that was waxing stronger every passing minute was rumour. There were rumours that our GOC was not only leaking our plans, but also giving our arms and ammunitions to the enemy. Having been almost dispossessed of Port-Harcourt, the enemy resistance was thought to have been completely broken. According to the rumours the enemy's continuous resistance was due to a sellout. The GOC was said to have some sympathies for the enemy, because either his mother or wife was said to be an Ibo, the ethnic group that was at the core of the secession.

We were ruminating over these rumours when the trucks came. They appeared from our fronts which in the real sense was no longer a front. Obigbo, as said earlier, had been occupied by a sister unit. It was supposed to be our next objective if things had turned out as we expected. Our rear was now not safe so it became the new front. Immediately the trucks came to a stop at the headquarters, our company, which was less by a platoon or so was ordered to get into them.
'Dem no go write names?' someone sighed.

I realized that since we came to the battlefronts there had been no writing or calling of names. In the rear, the names of soldiers going to fatigue in any place outside the immediate surroundings were written more than once. The orderly Corporal had to have a copy of such names. The Section Commander, another copy. The Platoon Sergeant and even the Company Sergeant-Major had to have their copies too. In the fronts, names of men for special and sometimes suicidal missions were often not recorded anywhere. Some men who were reported to have been drafted to Escravos during the Biafran occupation of the Mid-west were without army numbers. They were a few weeks old in their recruit training when this was said to have happened. If it is true; all those who lost their lives without numbers will have no records in the Army Headquarters.
'Why is the enemy bent on retaking Umuagbai instead of Afam since Afam is more important than Umuagbai?' I asked my trench mate once.
'Don't you know that the enemy would rather have the Afam Power Station remain intact in our hands than having it destroyed if protracted battles were fought there?'
'Ah, no wonder the place was abandoned to us without a serious fight,' I said.
'But, why do we want to retake Umuagbai? We could just leave it for them.' I asked again after a long pause.

'Afam itself will not be safe if Umuagbai is in the enemy's hands. Also, the Ogonis will not feel safe if Umuagbai, a neighbouring village, is in the hands of the Biafrans. In fact, our concentration point for the battles to retake Umuagbai was Bori, the headquarters of the Ogoni District.

To get to Bori from our position at Afam, we headed towards Obigbo along the tarred road. This was after we had been squeezed tightly into the trucks that had come to take us there. After covering some distance along the tarred road, the trucks swerved into a barely visible bush track by the left. We rumbled on through farms, groves and fallow land overgrown with bush. We swayed, docked and cried not only as a result of the speed of the vehicles by also because of the overhanging branches and bushes that lashed at us if we were a second too slow. After zigzagging for what seemed a long time, we burst out in another tarred road. We headed left on entering this road at the same breakneck speed. Traffic was heavy on this road compared to the ones we had been used to for sometime now. We passed three or four other military trucks that were grotesquely adorned with masks and motifs. Not long after, we arrived at Bori town. The emphasis placed on 'town' made it seem as if its 'township' was in dispute.

Before we sped into the town proper, we passed a sprawling green lawn crescented by blocks of houses. A few military hardware could be seen under the shades of the trees that were between these houses. Soldiers could also be seen moving in and out of the houses. As we learnt, the place was the most famous secondary school in the District. Now, it was serving as Headquarters to one of our sister Brigades with whom we started the march to Port Harcourt. The forward-men of this Brigade were now closing in on Port Harcourt. We forked into a side street by our left and in a few minutes, the trucks came to a stop in front of some dilapidated buildings. Here, we learnt, was the temporary headquarters of our Brigade since Umuagbai was wrenched from us by the enemy.

We were not allowed a minute's rest on getting down from the trucks. The journey was continued, this time on foot. We marched on along an untarred but motorable road. We crossed one rickety bridge which groaned and creaked under our weight.

Like amphibians, we had been sleeping in waterlogged trenches in the past few days at Afam. Rain or sunshine, we were always in the open since our second crossing. We started noticing the bad condition of our feet a few days after arriving in Calabar. Everywhere we stayed, if an attack was not imminent our other preoccupation if we were not foraging for what to eat, was the airing of our feet and footwears. After removing the canvas shoes or boots, we would squeeze water out of the socks before spreading them out to dry outside if it was not raining. After that, we would start peeling off whitish fluffy old skins that had formed in-between our toes. Few odours are as nauseating as that of dirty socks mixed with dirtier feet. We had been bearing our foot-rot because so far we have not embarked on any long march at a stretch. But on that day, as we were marching from Bori Town to the next point, the disease was aggravated. Worst affected were those wearing the canvas boots that were commonly called patrol or jungle boots. When the pains became too unbearable to allow for even a limp, some of us started removing the damned footwears and hung them, tied together by the laces on our shoulders or across the neck. Others whose footwears were already torn beyond use as a result of the trek, had them removed and thrown away. We limped like this until we arrived at a junction late in the night. Here we were told to pass the night.

We had not lain down for an hour when some of us were wakened by a deep eerie monotonous rumbling sound. We sat up in the darkness and pricked up our ears so as to place the frightening sound that seemed to be coming out of the bowels of the earth. Our efforts in this direction was in vain, for as soon as we sat up, the sound was not heard again. We were beginning to doubt our sanity for as soon as we lay down again, the sound became audible and even louder. This time before we could start a panicky shoot out with this mysterious enemy, we started to hear the noise faintly as we sat up. In our prone positions, the direction from which the sound was coming was not the same to all of us. Some claimed it was coming from our front. Others said it was from our sides or the rear. Nobody was however very certain of the direction claimed. All were based on guesswork. As soon as we started hearing the sound in our crouching positions, it became clear that it was coming from our rear.

To our utter relief, we realized that it was the sound of hundreds of boots of our colleagues as they were marching to join us. Before long, the dark awe-inspiring silhouette of the men at the vanguard of this march became visible. In a few minutes they arrived in our position.
